revision.text:
[[ja:強訴]][[zh:強訴]] '''Kōso''' (強訴) refers to actions taken to assert demands with a strong, uncompromising attitude. It is also known as "'''Gōso'''” (嗷訴). In Japan, the term specifically refers to collective demands or petitions made by {{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and shrine powers|qq=}} to the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷 (日本)|lt=Imperial Court|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|qid=Q30253177}} or {{ill|Shogunate|ja|幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogunate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shogunate|qid=Q1074150}}, backed by the authority of deities and military power, from the mid-{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} onward. It also refers to demands by farmers against their lords during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, such as petitions to reduce taxes.『大辞泉』「強訴」 == Kōso by Temple and Shrine Powers == Temple and shrine powers frequently resorted to force to have their demands met by the Imperial Court or shogunate. They often mobilized armed groups, such as {{ill|Shuto|ja|衆徒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuto|qq=}} (including {{ill|Sōhei|en|Sōhei|qid=Q1475695|lt=Sōhei|1=Sōhei}}) or {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinin|qq=}}. When issues arose concerning their temples or shrines, these warrior monks (sōhei) would don hoods called ''katō'' to cover their faces, alter their voices, and debate their cause. Those in favor would cry, "Indeed, indeed!" while dissenters would shout, "Unfounded!" Once a decision was made, no dissent was tolerated, and they acted immediately.{{Cite book|和書|author={{ill|Shimomukai Tatsuhiko|ja|下向井龍彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimomukai Tatsuhiko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|title=The Growth of Samurai and the Cloistered Rule Era in Japan's History|series={{ill|Kodansha Academic Library|ja|講談社学術文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kodansha Academic Library|1=Kōdansha gakujutsu bunko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11632220}}|isbn=978-4062919074|pages=242-243}} Prominent among these were {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|qid=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji|1=Kōfuku-ji}} in Nara and {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|qid=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji|1=Enryaku-ji}} on Mount Hiei, collectively referred to as "Nanto-Hokurei" (南都北嶺). Kōfuku-ji employed the {{ill|Sacred tree of Kasuga|ja|春日神木|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacred tree of Kasuga|1=Sacred tree of Kasuga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11513658}} from {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|qid=Q714559|lt=Kasuga-taisha|1=Kasuga-taisha}}, while Enryaku-ji used the {{ill|lt=Sacred palanquin|Mikoshi|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lt=Sacred palanquin|qq=}} of {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|qid=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha|1=Hiyoshi Taisha}} to wield "divine power" as they marched into Kyoto's Imperial Palace (洛中内裏). If their demands were not met, they would leave the sacred tree or palanquin in front of the palace gates, effectively halting government functions. The use of the sacred tree was termed "Sakaki-furi" (榊振り), while the sacred palanquin approach was called "Mikoshi-furi" (神輿振り).[https://books.google.co.jp/books?id=qRkGAQAAQBAJ&pg=PT184 'New Tale of the Heike', Volume 1] by Yoshikawa Eiji, Goma Books, 2013 The first recorded ''mikoshi-furi'' occurred in 1095.{{Cite journal|和書|author=Koyama Masayuki|title=Closure Practices in Medieval Temple-Shrines|url=https://seijo.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/897|journal=Journal of Folklore Studies|publisher=Seijo University|issue=11|date=1988-03|pages=61-77|id={{CRID|1050282812448951936}}}} The {{ill|The Tale of the Heike|en|The Tale of the Heike|qid=Q830740|lt=Heike Monogatari|1=The Tale of the Heike}} recounts an anecdote in which {{ill|Emperor Shirakawa|en|Emperor Shirakawa|qid=Q60949|lt=Emperor Shirakawa|1=Emperor Shirakawa}} lamented that "the waters of the {{ill|Kamogawa|en|Kamogawa|qid=Q31476|lt=Kamo River (Yodo River system)|1=Kamogawa}}, the roll of dice in {{ill|Sugoroku|ja|双六|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugoroku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to すごろく|1=Sugoroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1472906}}, and the warrior monks of Mount Hiei—these three things do not bend to my will," highlighting how even the emperor could not control these monks (referred to as the "Three Obstacles of the Realm"). In the case of Kōfuku-ji's ''Sakaki-furi'', the process began with moving the sacred tree from the main hall to a temporary shrine (御遷座). If demands were met, the sacred tree would be returned (御帰座). If not, it would be moved to the Golden Hall (金堂) at Kōfuku-ji. If still unresolved, the monks would march towards Kyoto, pausing first at {{ill|Kizugawa, Kyoto|en|Kizugawa, Kyoto|qid=Q990352|lt=Kizugawa|1=Kizugawa, Kyoto}} and then progressing to {{ill|Byōdō-in|en|Byōdō-in|qid=Q61094|lt=Byōdō-in|1=Byōdō-in}} in {{ill|Uji (disambiguation)|en|Uji (disambiguation)|qid=Q422616|lt=Uji, Kyoto|1=Uji (disambiguation)}} before finally entering Kyoto.{{Cite journal|和書|author=Tonami Miwako|date=2013-03|url=https://hdl.handle.net/10935/3373|title=The "Process of Carrying the Sacred Tree to Kyoto" in the Records of Kasuga Shrine Families|journal=Josetsu|ISSN=0386-359X|publisher=Nara Women's University|volume=40|pages=175-192|id={{CRID|1050282813368551808}}}} {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|qid=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}} also experienced similar acts of defiance. From the 10th century onward, priests and shrine attendants bypassed higher authorities and directly petitioned the court in Kyoto. By the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, these actions were termed "Jingū Taisho" (神宮大訴) and classified as a type of kōso or ''kosso'' (越訴).{{Citation|和書|author={{ill|Inaba Nobumichi|ja|稲葉伸道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba Nobumichi|1=Nobumichi Inaba|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11596905}}|chapter=Royal Policies for Shrines in the Mid-to-Late Kamakura Period|title=Japanese Medieval Royal and Shogunal Policies on Temples and Shrines|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2019|pages=120-125}} The reasons for kōso often involved disputes over shrine estates (荘園) being infringed upon by {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|qid=Q1351234|lt=Kokushi|1=Kokushi (official)}} or rival shrines receiving better treatment. To suppress kōso, the Imperial Court increasingly relied on the military power of {{ill|Samurai|ja|武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1975935}}, allowing them to gradually gain influence in central governance. Consequently, power in Japan became divided among the Imperial Court, the warrior class, and temple-shrine forces. Kōso by temples and shrines was prevalent from the Heian to the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} but declined with the weakening of temple-shrine authority. Simultaneously, the integration of Buddhism into the warrior class created a complex relationship between the two groups. === Timeline === {| class="wikitable" style="width: 100%;" |- !style="width: 4em;"|Japanese Era !style="width: 4em;"|Year (CE) !style="width: 5em;"|Date
([Lunisolar]) !style="width: 7em;"|Temple/Shrine !Description !style="width: 8em;"|Source |- |{{ill|Anna|en|Anna|qid=Q223958|lt=Anna|1=Anna}} 1 |{{ill|968|en|968|qid=Q23918|lt=968|1=968}} |July 15 |Kofukuji |Conflict with Todaiji over temple lands |Omiya Monjo |- |[[Tengen (Japan)|Tengen]] 4 |{{ill|981|en|981|qid=Q23420|lt=981|1=981}} |December 15 |Enryakuji |The Ennin faction demanded the dismissal of the Hossoji head priest, Yokai (of the Enchin faction) |[[Fusoryakki]] |- | rowspan="2" |{{ill|Kanwa|en|Kanwa|qid=Q16963511|lt=Kanwa|1=Kanwa}} 2 | rowspan="2" |{{ill|986|en|986|qid=Q23785|lt=986|1=986}} |February 26 |Kofukuji |Complaint about abuses by Bizen Governor Fujiwara Tokikane regarding Bizen Province's Kagata Sho |{{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|en|Nihon Kiryaku|qid=Q1053542|lt=Nihon Kiryaku|1=Nihon Kiryaku}} |- |July 13 |Kofukuji |Accusation against Yamato Governor Minamoto no Yorichika |[[Ichidai Yoki]] |- | rowspan="2" |[[:en:Kanko|Kanko]] 1 | rowspan="2" |{{ill|1004|en|1004|qid=Q5049|lt=1004|1=1004}} |February 26 |Sumiyoshi Shrine in Settsu |Complaint against Governor Fujiwara Setsuko for injuring shrine workers |[[Mido Kanpaku Ki]] |- |March 24 |Usa Hachiman Shrine |Complaint against abuses by Governor Fujiwara Koretada of the Dazaifu; Koretada's office was suspended on June 8 |Nihon Kiryaku |- |Kanko 3 |{{ill|1006|en|1006|qid=Q19232|lt=1006|1=1006}} |July 13 |Kofukuji |Petition to Fujiwara no Michinaga about crop failures in the province |Nihon Kiryaku |- |{{ill|Kannin|en|Kannin|qid=Q1193951|lt=Kannin|1=Kannin}} 1 |{{ill|1017|en|1017|qid=Q19249|lt=1017|1=1017}} |June 22 |Kofukuji |March to Kyoto carrying the sacred tree (reason unknown) |Omiya Monjo |- |{{ill|Manju|en|Manju|qid=Q1890496|lt=Manju|1=Manju}} 4 |{{ill|1027|en|1027|qid=Q19271|lt=1027|1=1027}} |April 26 |Enryakuji |Protest against the establishment of a nunnery at Hoshoji |[[Shoyu Ki]] |- |[[:en:Chogen|Chogen]] 1 |{{ill|1028|en|1028|qid=Q19273|lt=1028|1=1028}} |October 13 |Kinpusen |Complaint against oppressive governance by Yamato Governor Fujiwara no Yasumasa |[[Sakyo Ki]] |- |[[:en:Choryaku|Choryaku]] 3 |{{ill|1039|en|1039|qid=Q19341|lt=1039|1=1039}} |February 18 |Enryakuji |Protest against the appointment of Myoso (Temple Faction) to the Tendai Head Priest position by Fujiwara no Yorimichi. On March 16, mobs set fire to Yorimichi's Takayoin residence |Fusoryakki |- |{{ill|Jiryaku|en|Jiryaku|qid=Q934365|lt=Jiryaku|1=Jiryaku}} 2 |{{ill|1066|en|1066|qid=Q19411|lt=1066|1=1066}} |January 7 |Kofukuji |March to Kyoto carrying the sacred tree (reason unknown) |Omiya Monjo |- |[[:en:Shoryaku|Shoryaku]] 3 |{{ill|1079|en|1079|qid=Q19445|lt=1079|1=1079}} |June 2 |Enryakuji |Demand for the position of the Gion Shrine head priest |[[Tamefusa Kyoki]] |- |[[Eihou]] 1 |{{ill|1081|en|1081|qid=Q19450|lt=1081|1=1081}} |March 25 |Mount Tonomine |Complaint about Kofukuji's oppressive behavior |{{ill|Shuki|en|Shuki|qid=Q131114445|lt=Shuki|1=Shuki}} |- |Eihou 2 |{{ill|1082|en|1082|qid=Q19452|lt=1082|1=1082}} |March 25 |Kumano |Protest against the murder of Kumano shrine workers by retainers from Owari Province |Fusoryakki |- |[[:en:Otoku|Otoku]] 2 |{{ill|1085|en|1085|qid=Q19459|lt=1085|1=1085}} |July 20 |Kofukuji |Petition about the usurpation of temple estates |Tamefusa Kyoki |- |{{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|qid=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}} 2 |{{ill|1088|en|1088|qid=Q19463|lt=1088|1=1088}} |February 1 |Usa Hachiman Shrine |Complaint about the shooting of Usa shrine palanquin by former Dazaifu Governor Fujiwara no Sanemasa. Sanemasa was exiled to Izu on November 30 |[[Chuuki]] |- |} === Main Cases === * {{ill|Eikyū Petition|ja|永久の強訴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eikyū Petition|1=Eikyū Petition|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11549673}} * {{ill|Gion Riot Incident|ja|祇園闘乱事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Riot Incident|1=Gion Riot Incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11588449}} * {{ill|Kaō Petition|ja|嘉応の強訴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaō Petition|1=Kaō Petition|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11419399}} * {{ill|Hakusan Incident (Shishigatani Conspiracy)|ja|鹿ケ谷の陰謀#白山事件|lt=Hakusan Incident (Shishigatani Conspiracy)|lt_ja=白山事件（鹿ケ谷の陰謀）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Incident (Shishigatani Conspiracy)|1=Shishigatani incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q73140}} * {{ill|Petition of Kenkyū Year 2|ja|建久二年の強訴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Petition of Kenkyū Year 2|1=Gōso of 1191|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11486617}} == Petitions by Farmers == During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, collective protests by {{ill|Peasant|en|Peasant|qid=Q838811|lt=farmers|1=Peasant}} against the {{ill|authorities|ja|お上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/authorities|1=Okami (secular honorific)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11262868}} were referred to as petitions, known as {{ill|Hyakushō Ikki|ja|百姓一揆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakushō Ikki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一揆|1=ikki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8191556}} (peasant uprisings). These often sought the reduction or exemption of {{ill|annual tribute|ja|年貢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annual tribute|1=nengu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11483871}} or the replacement of {{ill|village officials|ja|村役人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/village officials|1=village officials|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q131940044}}. Especially during the {{ill|Bakumatsu period|ja|幕末期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakumatsu period|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 幕末|1=Bakumatsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q804558}}, {{ill|Yonaoshi Ikki|ja|世直し一揆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yonaoshi Ikki|1=yonaoshi-ikki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11362482}} (world-renewing uprisings) occurred frequently across Japan. In {{ill|Keiō|en|Keiō|qid=Q776775|lt=Keiō|1=Keiō}} 4 ({{ill|1868|en|1868|qid=Q7717|lt=1868|1=1868}}), around the same time as the {{ill|Charter Oath|en|Charter Oath|qid=Q702056|lt=Five Charter Oath|1=Charter Oath}} in March, the {{ill|Five Public Notices|en|Five Public Notices|qid=Q5456111|lt=Five Public Notices|1=Five Public Notices}} were issued by the Meiji government to ban collective protests, including {{ill|tōdō|ja|徒党|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tōdō|qq=}} (factions) and {{ill|escape of peasants|ja|逃散|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/escape of peasants|1=Fugitive peasants|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11639365}} (mass flight). However, this did not mean the populace complied meekly. Even during the Meiji period, movements such as the {{ill|Blood tax riots|en|Blood tax riots|qid=Q11625961|lt=Blood Tax Rebellion|1=Blood tax riots}} and the {{ill|Land Tax Revision Protests|ja|地租改正反対一揆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Land Tax Revision Protests|1=Land Tax Revision Protests|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11424654}} continued to emerge. == See Also == * {{ill|Buddhism and violence|en|Buddhism and violence|qid=Q17027057|lt=Buddhism and Violence|1=Buddhism and violence}} * {{ill|Criticism of Buddhism|en|Criticism of Buddhism|qid=Q2982106|lt=Criticism of Buddhism|1=Criticism of Buddhism}} * {{ill|Petitions|ja|越訴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Petitions|1=Petitions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11636601}} * {{ill|Direct Appeals|ja|直訴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Direct Appeals|1=jikiso|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11581842}} * {{ill|Temple Petitions|ja|門訴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple Petitions|1=Temple Petitions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11655105}} * {{ill|Demonstrations|ja|デモ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Demonstrations|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to デモ活動|1=Political demonstration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q175331}} * {{ill|Self-help (law)|en|Self-help (law)|qid=Q702275|lt=Self-Redress|1=Self-help (law)}} * {{ill|Rokosz|en|Rokosz|qid=Q1325214|lt=Rokosz|1=Rokosz}} - Petitions in Poland, based on the {{ill|Right to resist|en|Right to resist|qid=Q708363|lt=Right of Resistance|1=Right to resist}}. == External Links == * {{Cite journal|和書|author=Asako Ueno, Akihiro Kitamura, Satoshi Kuroda, Tomomi Nishio |date=2011-03 |url=https://waseda.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/27252 |title=Introduction and Reproduction of "Records of Repeated Riots during Sacred Tree Movements" |journal=Waseda University Advanced Research Bulletin |ISSN=1883-5163 |publisher=Waseda University Advanced Research Institute |volume=3 |pages=106-91 |hdl=2065/39450 |naid=120005300819 |CRID=1050001202461541760 |ref=harv}} * {{Cite journal|和書|author=Hiromitsu Moriya |date=2011-04 |url=https://kwansei.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/18017 |title=Punishment of Riot and Petitions during the Bunka Era in Morioka Domain |journal=Law and Politics |ISSN=02880709 |publisher=Kwansei Gakuin University Law and Politics Society |volume=62 |issue=1 (Part 2) |pages=117(575)-139(597) |hdl=10236/7689 |naid=40018966979 |CRID=1050845763826612224}} * {{Cite journal|和書|author=Masahiro Hata |date=1984-10 |url=http://bud.beppu-u.ac.jp/modules/xoonips/detail.php?id=kc11505 |title="Hōei Petition" and "Hōju-ji Village Dispute" in the Funai Domain |journal=Ōita Prefecture Local History |ISSN=0287-6809 |publisher=Ōita Prefecture Local History Research Society |volume=115 |pages=63-73 |naid=120005265640 |CRID=1050845762586250368}} == References == == Bibliography == * {{ill|Kuroda Toshio|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Toshio|qq=}} . *Temple and Shrine Powers: Another Medieval Society.* Iwanami Shinsho, 1980. * {{ill|Itō Masatoshi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itō Masatoshi|qq=}} . *The Medieval Religious Powers: Migration and Connection.* Chikuma Shinsho, 2008. * == Related Literature == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Katsuno Takanobu|title=Warrior Monks|publisher={{ill|Shibundō|ja|至文堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibundō|1=Shibundo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11613176}}|series=New Japanese History Series|year=1955}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hioki Eigō|title=History of Warrior Monks|publisher={{ill|Jukōshō Shuppan|ja|戎光祥出版|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jukōshō Shuppan|1=Jukōshō Shuppan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11495343}}|year=2003}} * * }} {{translated page|ja|強訴|version=104385321|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[ja:強訴]] [[ja:黒田俊雄]] [[ja:伊藤正敏]] [[ja:顕密体制]] [[ja:権門体制]]
